description:
You are working as a web-developer who has just cloned a project into a
    fresh Linux environment.  
    For your local tests you must prepare a very small directory structure
    and enforce the correct Unix permissions, so that:

        1. The project’s root directory can be entered and listed only by you
           and members of your default group.
        2. The “public” directory (which later will hold static assets) can be
           freely read by everyone but written only by you.

    REQUIRED FINAL STATE
    --------------------
    • Two directories must exist exactly at the following absolute paths  
      (create any parent directories that do not yet exist):
        - /home/user/my_site
        - /home/user/my_site/public

    • Permissions (octal) must be set precisely to:  
        - 750 for /home/user/my_site   (rwx r-x ---)  
        - 755 for /home/user/my_site/public   (rwx r-x r-x)

    • A plain-text log file named /home/user/permission_snapshot.log must be
      produced.  Its content is used by automated tests, so its format
      must be **exactly** as specified:

          Line 1  →  "750 /home/user/my_site"
          Line 2  →  "755 /home/user/my_site/public"

      (Each line must end in a single UNIX newline “\n”, and there must be no
      extra whitespace before, between or after the two fields.)

    • The two numbers are the octal permission modes; the single space is a
      literal space character; the paths must be absolute and must not be
      resolved to relative or tilde notation.

    HINTS (do not reveal commands directly):
      • Think of the smallest set of shell commands (2–3 is enough) that can
        build the directory tree, apply the two different permission modes,
        and record the required information into the log file in the required
        format.
      • One typical approach is to create the directories, adjust their modes,
        then use a tool that can print “<mode> <path>” lines so you can redirect
        that output into the log file.

    When everything is done correctly the automated checker will:
      1. verify that both directories exist;
      2. confirm their permission bits are 750 and 755, respectively;
      3. read /home/user/permission_snapshot.log and compare its two lines to
         the exact strings shown above.

    You may print any console output you find helpful while you work, but only
    the log file’s content will be graded, so make sure it adheres 100 % to
    the required format.
truth:
EXPECTED STRUCTURE & PERMISSIONS
    --------------------------------
    Path: /home/user/my_site
      type: directory
      octal_mode: 750
    Path: /home/user/my_site/public
      type: directory
      octal_mode: 755

    LOG FILE
    --------
    File: /home/user/permission_snapshot.log
    Expected exact byte content (including newlines):
        750 /home/user/my_site\n
        755 /home/user/my_site/public\n
